g.data-narration: Welcome to learning the Trapezoidal Rule in MPI - a fundamental parallel computing technique.
Numerical integration approximates definite integrals when analytical solutions are difficult or impossible to obtain.
g.text: Approximating definite integrals when analytical solutions are difficult or impossible.
Essential technique in computational mathematics, physics, engineering, and finance.

g.data-narration: The trapezoidal rule approximates the integral by dividing the area into trapezoids with width h=(b-a)/n.
g.text: where h = (b-a)/n is the step size

g.data-narration: We parallelize numerical integration when high precision is needed, the function is expensive, or multiple integrals are required.
Domain decomposition divides the interval [a,b] among processes. Each process computes on its own subinterval independently.
Every MPI program starts with MPI_Init to initialize the parallel environment and get process rank and size.
MPI_Bcast broadcasts input parameters a, b, and n from the root process to all other processes in the communicator.
Each process computes its local integral using the trapezoidal formula on its subinterval independently.
MPI_Reduce with MPI_SUM aggregates all local integrals into the final result at the root process.

g.text: Reduce operation combines all local results

g.data-narration: Here's the complete MPI trapezoidal rule program structure showing initialization, computation, and finalization.

g.text: MPI_Init → MPI_Comm_rank/size → Read a,b,n (rank 0) → MPI_Bcast → local_integral = trapezoidal()
int my_rank, num_procs; double a, b; int n;

g.data-narration: When n is not evenly divisible by p, distribute extra subintervals to the first (n mod p) processes for load balancing.
The trapezoidal rule has error O(h²), meaning doubling n reduces error by factor of 4.
Parallel time complexity is O(n/p + log p), where n is subintervals and p is number of processes.
Key takeaways: domain decomposition, local computation, MPI_Bcast for parameters, MPI_Reduce for aggregation.
Remember the formula, MPI operations, edge cases, complexity analysis, and comparison with other methods for exams.
